refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable operators and the system to achieve and maintain energy efficiency performance such that measured energy_per_ton (energy_used / ton_moved) remains at or below the customer-defined E_target throughout the evaluation period, under nominal operating conditions and across the range of supported worksite scenarios.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-003 is a high-level KPI requirement that must be decomposed into measurable, controllable functional requirements. The refinement clarifies that energy efficiency is a system-level property emerging from multiple subsystems (propulsion, hydraulics, teleoperation overhead, idle management, load optimization). The requirement now explicitly bounds the evaluation context (nominal conditions, evaluation period, worksite scenarios) to enable testability and to distinguish energy efficiency from safety or availability concerns.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Establish a baseline energy consumption model (kWh per ton) under controlled worksite conditions. Instrument the vehicle with power metering at major subsystems (engine, hydraulic pump, electric drivetrain, teleoperation link). Conduct acceptance testing over a representative mission profile; calculate energy_per_ton at regular intervals and confirm it does not exceed E_target for ≥95% of the evaluation period.
sub_frs: FR-EE-001: The propulsion subsystem shall minimize energy loss during power transmission and load movement.
FR-EE-002: The hydraulic/actuation subsystem shall minimize parasitic losses and idle consumption.
FR-EE-003: The teleoperation subsystem shall minimize network and processing overhead energy consumption.
FR-EE-004: The vehicle control system shall optimize load scheduling and idle state management to reduce unnecessary energy expenditure.
FR-EE-005: The NGHE solution shall provide real-time energy consumption telemetry to enable operator awareness and decision-making.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement a centralized energy management controller that monitors subsystem power draw, predicts energy consumption against mission profile, and dynamically throttles non-critical loads (hydraulics, teleoperation fidelity) to stay within energy budget.
DP-B: Optimize each subsystem independently (efficient motor selection, hydraulic circuit redesign, network compression) and rely on operator discipline to achieve overall energy target.

DP-C: Implement a hybrid approach: establish energy budgets per subsystem (propulsion, hydraulics, teleoperation, idle management), deploy local controllers in each subsystem to enforce budgets, and provide a system-level energy dashboard for operator feedback and mission planning.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-C: Implement a hybrid approach with subsystem-level energy budgets, local enforcement controllers, and a system-level energy dashboard. This approach balances autonomous energy management (via local controllers) with operator visibility and control, reducing coupling between subsystems while maintaining system-level KPI accountability.

potential_sources: Subsystem energy budgets must be coordinated to sum to the overall energy_per_ton target; misalignment between budget allocation and actual subsystem efficiency could cause overshoot.
Teleoperation subsystem energy consumption depends on network conditions and operator control frequency, which are not fully deterministic; budget must include margin.
Load scheduling and idle management decisions in the vehicle control system affect propulsion and hydraulic energy; these must be coordinated with subsystem budgets.
coupling_check.notes: DP-C introduces a coordination layer (energy dashboard and budget allocation) but avoids tight coupling by allowing each subsystem to manage its own budget independently. The risk is that budget allocation is a design-time decision; if actual subsystem efficiency differs from assumptions, the system may not meet the KPI. Mitigation: include adaptive budget reallocation logic and operator override capability.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Subsystem energy efficiency can be characterized and budgeted independently. Risk: Interactions between subsystems (e.g., hydraulic pressure ripple affecting propulsion efficiency) may violate independence assumption.
Assumption: Operator will use energy dashboard to make informed decisions. Risk: Operator may prioritize task completion over energy efficiency, especially under time pressure.
Assumption: Network conditions are stable enough to predict teleoperation energy overhead. Risk: Congested or high-latency networks could increase teleoperation energy consumption unpredictably.
Assumption: E_target is achievable with current technology and worksite conditions. Risk: Customer may set unrealistic target; validation required early.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-C to explicitly include: (1) a subsystem energy model with sensitivity analysis to identify critical efficiency drivers, (2) an adaptive budget reallocation algorithm triggered when any subsystem approaches its budget limit, (3) a fallback mode that reduces teleoperation fidelity or load scheduling complexity if energy consumption exceeds threshold, and (4) a validation gate to confirm E_target feasibility before full system integration.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: The NGHE Solution module is the top-level system boundary that integrates Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services subsystems. It owns the system-level energy management policy, budget allocation logic, energy dashboard, and the coordination interfaces between subsystems. The module is responsible for ensuring that the combined energy consumption of all subsystems remains within the energy_per_ton target.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Energy consumption data (kWh, ton_moved, energy_per_ton, subsystem power draw, budget status) is transmitted via Telemetry to Site Manager and Tele-operator for visibility and decision support.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Mission tasks (load movement, positioning, idle periods) are received via Tasking interface from Site Manager or autonomous planner; energy management system uses task profile to predict and allocate energy budgets.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: If energy consumption trajectory indicates the system will exceed E_target, or if energy budget exhaustion would compromise safety-critical functions, SafetyAlert is triggered to notify Safety Manager and operator.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Energy management system monitors Network Link utilization and overhead to account for teleoperation energy consumption in the overall budget.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the natural top-level owner of system-level energy management policy, budget allocation, and the energy dashboard. All energy-related coordination logic resides here. Minimal diffusion: only subsystem-level energy controllers (owned by Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) are external to this module.

Vehicle owns propulsion and hydraulic subsystems, which are major energy consumers. However, energy budget allocation and system-level coordination are not Vehicle's responsibility; these belong to NGHE Solution. If energy management were assigned to Vehicle, it would require Vehicle to coordinate with Teleoperation and Safety Services, creating diffusion and coupling.

Teleoperation owns network link and remote control processing, which consume energy. However, Teleoperation should not own system-level energy policy or budget allocation; that is NGHE Solution's responsibility. Assigning energy management to Teleoperation would create tight coupling with Vehicle and Safety Services.

Safety Services owns safety-critical monitoring and alerts. Energy efficiency is not a safety function per se, but energy exhaustion could compromise safety (e.g., loss of hydraulic pressure for emergency stop). Safety Services should monitor energy status and trigger SafetyAlert if needed, but should not own the energy management policy itself.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The NGHE Solution module is the appropriate owner of the energy efficiency KPI because: (1) energy efficiency is a system-level property that emerges from the interaction of multiple subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services); (2) the energy management policy (budget allocation, adaptive reallocation, fallback modes) must be coordinated across subsystems, which is a system-level concern; (3) the energy dashboard and operator feedback loop are system-level artifacts; (4) assigning energy management to any single subsystem (Vehicle, Teleoperation, or Safety Services) would create diffusion and coupling, violating the independence principle.
containment_risks: Risk: NGHE Solution may become a 'god module' that owns too many concerns (energy, safety, teleoperation, vehicle control). Mitigation: clearly delineate NGHE Solution's responsibility to energy policy and coordination; delegate subsystem-level energy control to Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services.

Risk: Energy budget allocation is a design-time decision that may not adapt to runtime variations in subsystem efficiency or worksite conditions. Mitigation: implement adaptive budget reallocation and operator override capability.

Risk: Telemetry interface may expose sensitive energy data that could be exploited to infer operator behavior or mission details. Mitigation: apply data filtering and access control policies to Telemetry output.

Risk: If energy management logic is centralized in NGHE Solution, a failure in the energy controller could disable the entire system. Mitigation: implement redundancy and fallback modes in the energy management subsystem.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Refine the IM assignment to explicitly define the boundary between NGHE Solution (system-level energy policy and coordination) and subsystem-level energy controllers (owned by Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services). Create a detailed interface specification for energy budget requests, status updates, and adaptive reallocation commands. Establish a clear ownership model for the energy dashboard and operator feedback loop.

fr_dp_gate.reason: The refined FR clearly specifies the energy efficiency KPI (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target) and the evaluation context (nominal conditions, evaluation period, worksite scenarios). DP-C (hybrid approach with subsystem budgets and system-level coordination) directly addresses the FR by enabling both autonomous energy management and operator visibility. The DP is feasible, testable, and aligns with the high safety criticality constraint (energy exhaustion could compromise safety-critical functions).

diffusion_risks: Subsystem-level energy controllers (in Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) must implement the energy budget enforcement logic; if these controllers are not properly coordinated, energy consumption could exceed the target. Mitigation: establish a clear interface specification and validation protocol for subsystem energy controllers.

The energy dashboard and operator feedback loop are owned by NGHE Solution but depend on real-time data from subsystems; latency or data loss could degrade operator decision-making. Mitigation: implement redundant telemetry paths and data validation.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is the appropriate owner of the energy management policy and coordination logic. Subsystem-level energy controllers are owned by Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services, which reduces diffusion and maintains clear ownership boundaries. The energy dashboard is a system-level artifact owned by NGHE Solution, which is appropriate for a KPI that spans multiple subsystems.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: The refined FR is verifiable through instrumentation of the vehicle with power metering at major subsystems and calculation of energy_per_ton over the evaluation period. The energy dashboard provides real-time visibility into energy consumption, enabling continuous verification during operation. Acceptance testing can be conducted over a representative mission profile.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: The allocation of energy efficiency responsibility to NGHE Solution is clear and justified. Subsystem-level energy controllers are allocated to Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services, with clear interfaces for budget enforcement and status reporting. The energy dashboard is allocated to NGHE Solution as a system-level artifact.

recommended_revisions: Establish a detailed interface specification for energy budget requests, status updates, and adaptive reallocation commands between NGHE Solution and subsystem-level energy controllers.
Define the energy model and budget allocation algorithm at the NGHE Solution level, including sensitivity analysis to identify critical efficiency drivers and adaptive reallocation logic.
Specify the energy dashboard requirements, including data sources, update frequency, and operator interaction patterns.
Establish a validation gate to confirm E_target feasibility before full system integration, including subsystem efficiency characterization and worksite condition analysis.
Define fallback modes and operator override capabilities to handle energy budget exhaustion or subsystem efficiency degradation.

decision.justification: The refined FR is clear, verifiable, and well-scoped. The selected DP (hybrid approach with subsystem budgets and system-level coordination) is feasible and addresses the FR. The assigned IM (NGHE Solution) is appropriate and justified. However, the FR is not atomic; it decomposes into five sub-FRs (propulsion efficiency, hydraulic efficiency, teleoperation overhead, load scheduling, and energy telemetry). To proceed with detailed design, each sub-FR must be decomposed to the next level, with corresponding DP selection and IM assignment. This is a ZIGZAG_DOWN decision: proceed to decompose the sub-FRs while maintaining traceability to the parent FR and DP.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-EE-001: The propulsion subsystem shall minimize energy loss during power transmission and load movement.
FR-EE-002: The hydraulic/actuation subsystem shall minimize parasitic losses and idle consumption.
FR-EE-003: The teleoperation subsystem shall minimize network and processing overhead energy consumption.
FR-EE-004: The vehicle control system shall optimize load scheduling and idle state management to reduce unnecessary energy expenditure.
FR-EE-005: The NGHE solution shall provide real-time energy consumption telemetry to enable operator awareness and decision-making.

assumptions: Subsystem energy efficiency can be characterized and budgeted independently, with acceptable interaction effects.
Operator will use energy dashboard to make informed decisions; operator discipline is a factor in achieving the KPI.
Network conditions are stable enough to predict teleoperation energy overhead within acceptable margins.
E_target is achievable with current technology and worksite conditions; validation required early.
Energy management policy can be implemented as a coordination layer without tight coupling to subsystem control logic.
Subsystem-level energy controllers can enforce budgets autonomously without requiring real-time coordination with NGHE Solution.
open_risks: E_target feasibility: Customer may set unrealistic target; early validation required.
Subsystem interaction effects: Hydraulic pressure ripple, propulsion efficiency variation, and teleoperation latency may violate independence assumptions.
Operator behavior: Operator may prioritize task completion over energy efficiency, especially under time pressure.
Network variability: Congested or high-latency networks could increase teleoperation energy consumption unpredictably.
Adaptive budget reallocation: Algorithm complexity and real-time performance must be validated.
Safety criticality: Energy exhaustion could compromise safety-critical functions; fallback modes and redundancy required.
evidence_needed: Subsystem energy models with sensitivity analysis to identify critical efficiency drivers.
Characterization of subsystem efficiency under nominal and off-nominal conditions.
Network energy overhead model for teleoperation subsystem.
Worksite condition analysis to establish representative mission profiles for acceptance testing.
Validation of E_target feasibility through simulation or prototype testing.
Specification of adaptive budget reallocation algorithm and fallback modes.
Safety analysis to identify energy exhaustion scenarios and mitigation strategies.
Operator interface design for energy dashboard and feedback loop.
